metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the CODEOWNERS exclusion rationale: replace 'rebar bot-closes' GitHub PRs with the accurate mechanism — a repository ruleset blocks PR merges to `main` (confirmed in CLAUDE.md and pull_request_template.md), making CODEOWNERS dead config regardless of whether PRs are auto-closed.
findings.finding: The plan's CODEOWNERS exclusion rationale states 'rebar bot-closes' GitHub PRs, but no workflow or bot that automatically closes GitHub PRs can be found in the codebase. The actual mechanism is a repository ruleset that rejects PR merges — PRs can remain open indefinitely. The stated mechanism ('bot-closes') is an unverified codebase assertion used to justify a scope exclusion.
findings.location: Scope / OUT section: 'CODEOWNERS (either flavor)' — rationale: 'it only auto-requests reviewers and gates merges on GitHub PRs, which rebar bot-closes'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'which rebar bot-closes' with 'which a GitHub repository ruleset prevents from merging into main' — this is the actual enforced mechanism and is verifiable in the codebase.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC4 (or the What section) the authoritative location for each of the three decisions — e.g., BDFL rationale prose belongs in GOVERNANCE.md Overview AND a ticket comment, CC version decision recorded in CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md header comment AND ticket, CODEOWNERS rationale in Scope/OUT of GOVERNANCE.md AND ticket — so the verify step is unambiguous.
findings.finding: AC4 specifies decisions recorded 'on the ticket,' but the What section implies the lead-maintainer-vs-BDFL decision is recorded in GOVERNANCE.md itself ('decision recorded' parenthetical), and leaves the CC 2.1 vs 3.0 location unspecified — creating ambiguity about which artifact an implementer should verify against when closing AC4.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4; What section items 1 and 'Assess the CC 2.1 → 3.0 upgrade'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC4 to: 'Three decisions recorded both in the relevant document and on the ticket: (a) lead-maintainer vs BDFL — rationale prose in GOVERNANCE.md §Overview; (b) CC 2.1 vs 3.0 — outcome noted in CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md header or GOVERNANCE.md changelog note; (c) CODEOWNERS non-goal — rationale in GOVERNANCE.md §Scope or a dedicated ADR comment on the ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Persist the three decision rationales (BDFL vs. lead-maintainer, CC 2.1 vs. 3.0, CODEOWNERS skip) inside the repository — either as inline notes in GOVERNANCE.md or as a lightweight `docs/decisions/` ADR — not only in the external ticket.
findings.finding: The three governance decisions (BDFL vs. lead-maintainer language, CC 2.1 vs. 3.0, CODEOWNERS non-goal rationale) are directed to be 'recorded on the ticket' (AC item 4) rather than persisted inside the repository itself. Ticket systems can become inaccessible, issues get closed and archived, and future contributors cloning the repo will have no in-repo record of WHY these choices were made.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — item 4: 'Three decisions recorded on the ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Design decisions' or 'Rationale' subsection at the bottom of GOVERNANCE.md (or a `docs/decisions/0001-governance-bootstrap.md` ADR) capturing: (1) lead-maintainer chosen over BDFL for role-language longevity; (2) CC version decision with outcome; (3) CODEOWNERS skipped because GitHub PRs are bot-closed and Gerrit code-owners is redundant with a single maintainer. Reference the ticket number for full thread context.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `SUPPORT.md` to the edit set (or note it as an explicit non-goal): update line 31's inline enforcement contact reference to match the new role-based wording in CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, or replace it with a link to the CoC enforcement section to avoid future drift.
findings.finding: SUPPORT.md line 31 hard-codes the CoC enforcement contact inline ('enforcement contact: hello@navapbc.com') and is not enumerated as a file to update when CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md's enforcement contact changes to the new role-based wording.
findings.location: What section / CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md edit target; SUPPORT.md:31
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope / What section, add: 'SUPPORT.md — update the inline CoC enforcement contact note (line 31) to either mirror the new role-based wording or replace the inline text with a pointer to CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md#enforcement to keep a single source of truth.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a conditional Section-6 template to the plan (two variants: 'authority rests with lead maintainer personally' vs 'authority rests with Nava PBC') so the executor can draft the full file now and swap in the correct variant once Nava confirms.
findings.finding: GOVERNANCE.md Section 6 (Nava's role / succession) cannot be written definitively because AC5a — 'whether authority rests with the person or with Nava PBC' — is unresolved and the plan provides no interim placeholder text or conditional template the executor can draft and finalize once the answer arrives. An executor will be blocked on what to write.
findings.location: What section, bullet 6 (Nava PBC's role); Acceptance Criteria AC5(a)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan under Section 6: 'If Nava confirms org-authority: … If individual-authority: … Nava's answer gates which variant merges.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete placeholder token (e.g., `security@navapbc.com` pending confirmation) and note it must be replaced before merge, or gate the PR on Nava's written confirmation of the backstop contact.
findings.finding: CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md (AC3) requires a 'named Nava backstop contact' but AC5b leaves that name/email unresolved, and the plan gives no placeholder format or conditional instruction, so the executor cannot produce a shippable CoC file.
findings.location: What section, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md bullet; Acceptance Criteria AC3 and AC5(b)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default decision in the plan (e.g., 'Default: keep CC 2.1; upgrade to 3.0 only if the diff is trivial') so the executor can act without a round-trip.
findings.finding: The CC 2.1 → 3.0 upgrade decision is deferred to the executor without a stated default; 'nice-to-have, not a blocker' implies staying on 2.1 but the plan does not say so explicitly, leaving the executor to guess the preferred outcome.
findings.location: What section, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md bullet ('Assess the CC 2.1 → 3.0 upgrade')
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Default decision: retain CC 2.1 for this ticket; upgrade to 3.0 can be a follow-on if the diff is non-trivial.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete completion gate for AC5: either a named Nava contact and a deadline (e.g., 'Nava must confirm in writing via ticket comment by [date]'), or split AC5 into a separate blocking prerequisite ticket so this ticket's in-session completability is self-contained.
findings.finding: AC5 ('Open org decisions resolved with Nava before close') depends on an external party (Nava PBC) with no stated timeline, escalation path, or fallback if Nava does not respond before the session ends, making in-session completability of the ticket as a whole uncertain.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the proving artifact for AC5 (e.g., 'Nava stakeholder posts confirmation comment on this ticket, or links a Slack/email thread') so the criterion is verifiable by any reviewer.
findings.finding: AC5 ('Open org decisions resolved with Nava before close') has no concrete verifiable artifact specified — the plan does not define what 'resolved' looks like (email, ticket comment from a Nava representative, a signed document), so reviewers cannot confirm the criterion is satisfied.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5: 'Resolved = Nava stakeholder posts a confirming comment on this ticket (or links written confirmation) for each of (a) and (b) before the PR merges.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to GOVERNANCE.md (or MAINTAINERS.md change-process note) a statement that the Nava CoC backstop contact is reviewed and updated whenever the Nava-side point of contact changes, and identify who at Nava owns triggering that update.
findings.finding: The Nava backstop CoC contact has no defined update-ownership lifecycle. The plan names it as an open org decision to resolve before close, but does not specify who is responsible for updating CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md when that contact changes, nor how consumers (reporters) are protected from staleness.
findings.location: What > CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md bullet; AC item (b) — 'the named Nava CoC backstop contact'
findings.suggested_fix: In the CoC section or MAINTAINERS.md change-process pointer, add: 'The Nava backstop contact in CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md is owned by Nava PBC; Nava is responsible for notifying the lead maintainer when it changes so the file can be updated via the normal Gerrit process.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC with a concrete validation mechanism for the outsider beneficiary — e.g., 'the named Nava CoC backstop contact is verified reachable before close' or 'a first-time contributor (or author acting as one) reads GOVERNANCE.md cold and can answer: who to contact, how to become a maintainer, where to file a report about the lead maintainer.'
findings.finding: No AC carries a concrete validation mechanism for the outsider beneficiary — all acceptance criteria are structural presence checks (file exists, section present, decision recorded), with no before/after workflow comparison, dogfooding step, or observable signal that the governance artifacts actually solve the stated problem for their audience.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC list: '- [ ] Validation: verify the Nava CoC backstop address/contact is live and responsive before closing; optionally, have one person unfamiliar with the project read GOVERNANCE.md and confirm the three outsider questions (decision authority, maintainer path, CoC escalation) are answerable without follow-up.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify GOVERNANCE.md Section 7 to specify that 'lead maintainer approves' means the lead maintainer submits changes through the normal two-vote Gerrit gate (bots included), and note whether emergency governance changes can use the GitHub mirror escape hatch documented in CONTRIBUTING.md.
findings.finding: GOVERNANCE.md Section 7 ('Changing this document — via the normal Gerrit review process, lead maintainer approves') will state that the lead maintainer's approval is sufficient to change the governance document, but in the actual Gerrit mechanism both LLM-Review +1 AND Verified +1 from bots are required before any submitter (including the lead maintainer) can land a change. A reader of GOVERNANCE.md will believe the lead maintainer has unilateral approval authority over governance changes, but in practice the bots co-approve every change. This creates a correctness mismatch between the governance statement and the actual workflow, which is especially visible for urgent governance edits (e.g., a CoC contact update) that could be blocked by the LLM-Review bot.
findings.suggested_fix: Reword Section 7 to: 'Changes to this document follow the same Gerrit review process as all other changes (LLM-Review + Verified required); the lead maintainer has final say on content and submits the change once both gates pass. For urgent changes where the normal gate is unavailable, the emergency escape hatch in CONTRIBUTING.md §3 applies.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
